home.title: Wepisia
home.quote: “Knowledge is like a baobab: no one person can embrace it.”
home.twoByTwoDesc: Contingency analysis, odds ratio, and relative risk.
home.biostatisticsDesc: From power calculations to Mantel‑Haenszel tests, access a suite of scientifically validated tools.
home.aiDescription: Interpret your results automatically with our contextual intelligence engine.
home.geospatialDescription: Visualize epidemiological events on interactive maps with dynamic heatmaps and intelligent clusters.
home.simulationDescription: Simulate complex epidemiological scenarios with our advanced simulation engine for optimal performance.

command.searchPlaceholder: Search for a module or test…

settings.title: Settings
settings.customizeApp: Customize the application according to your preferences
settings.reduceAnimationsDescription: Disable or simplify transitions and animations
settings.highContrastDescription: Increase color contrast for better readability
settings.compactModeDescription: Reduce margins and padding to display more content
settings.enableNotificationsDescription: Show toast notifications in the app
settings.soundNotificationsDescription: Play a sound when a notification appears
settings.hapticFeedbackDescription: Vibrate the device on notifications (if supported)
settings.settingsSaved: Settings are automatically saved in your browser.

stdMortalityCalculator.title: SMR Calculator
stdMortalityCalculator.subtitle: Epidemiology and mortality statistics.
stdMortalityCalculator.footnote: * For small counts (observed ≤ 5), prefer exact methods (Fisher, Mid‑P).
stdMortalityCalculator.jstatMissing: jStat not detected – exact intervals and p‑values replaced by approximations.
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationNotSignif: The difference is not statistically significant (CI contains 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.reportFooter: SMR Calculator – Statistical tool for epidemiology
stdMortalityCalculator.helpPrinciple: The SMR compares observed deaths in a specific population to expected deaths if that population had the same mortality rates as the general population.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpCi: The CI indicates the reliability of the result. If it contains the value 1, the result is not statistically significant.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpExactDesc: Based on Poisson distribution and χ² law. Reference for small counts.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpMidPDesc: Continuity correction of the exact test, less conservative.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpByarDesc: Rothman & Boice (1979) approximation, very accurate even for O > 5.

introduction.subtitle: Complete guide to biostatistics and simulation modules.

stdMortalityCalculator.title: SMR Calculator
stdMortalityCalculator.subtitle: Epidemiology and mortality statistics.
stdMortalityCalculator.footnote: * For small counts (observed ≤ 5), prefer exact methods (Fisher, Mid‑P).
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationSignifHigh: The excess is statistically significant (CI entirely > 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationSignifLow: The reduction is statistically significant (CI entirely < 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.interpretationNotSignif: The difference is not statistically significant (CI contains 1.0).
stdMortalityCalculator.helpPrinciple: The Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) is the ratio of observed deaths in the study population to the number of deaths expected if that population had the same mortality rates as the reference population.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpCi: A CI that does not include 1.0 indicates a statistically significant difference at the chosen level.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpExactDesc: Garwood's exact CI (1936) based on χ² law. Two‑sided exact Poisson test. OpenEpi reference method.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpMidPDesc: Lancaster's Mid‑P test (1961). Less conservative than the exact test. CI calculated by numerical bisection.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpByarDesc: Byar's cubic approximation (Rothman & Boice, 1979). Reliable for obs ≥ 1. p‑value by CI inversion.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpRgDesc: Log‑normal CI: exp(ln(SMR) ± z/√obs). Rothman & Greenland (1998). Undefined for obs = 0.
stdMortalityCalculator.helpChi2Desc: Pearson χ² test (1 df). No CI provided (consistent with OpenEpi).
stdMortalityCalculator.reportFooter: Automatically generated report — for epidemiological purposes only.

twoByTwoTable.title: 2×2 Tables
twoByTwoTable.description: Assessment of association between exposure and disease.
twoByTwoTable.explanation: 2×2 tables cross an exposure (yes/no) with a disease (yes/no). Produced statistics include Fisher's exact and mid‑p tests, chi‑square, odds ratio, relative risk, risk difference, and etiological fractions.

proportions.title: Proportions
proportions.description: Estimation of a proportion and its confidence interval.
proportions.exactClopperPearson: • Exact (Clopper‑Pearson): Conservative, based on binomial distribution
proportions.midPExact: • Exact Mid‑P: Less conservative than Clopper‑Pearson
proportions.useCaseDescription: Out of 500 tested individuals, 75 are positive. What is the true prevalence?

proportionsSample.title: Sample Size for a Proportion
proportionsSample.description: Calculates the sample size needed to estimate a proportion.
proportionsSample.explanation: This module determines the number of subjects required to estimate a proportion with a given precision.
proportionsSample.exampleDescription: To estimate a prevalence of 20% with ±3% precision and 95% confidence:

oneRate.title: Single Rate Test
oneRate.description: Inference on an incidence or mortality rate.
oneRate.explanation: Compares an observed rate to a reference rate or calculates its confidence interval. Uses Poisson distribution for rare events.
oneRate.exampleDescription: In a cohort of 10,000 person‑years, 25 cases are observed.

compareTwoRates.title: Comparison of Two Rates
compareTwoRates.description: Test of equality of two incidence rates.
compareTwoRates.explanation: Compares two rates based on person‑time data. Calculates rate ratio and rate difference with confidence intervals, as well as the Mantel‑Haenszel test.

anova.title: One‑way ANOVA
anova.description: Comparison of means of three or more groups.
anova.explanation: Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) tests the null hypothesis that the means of several groups are equal. It partitions total variance into between‑group and within‑group variance.
anova.anovaTable: ANOVA table: Sum of squares, degrees of freedom, mean squares

tTest.title: Student's t‑test
tTest.explanation: The t‑test compares means of two independent groups. Two versions are provided: Student's t‑test (equal variances) and Welch's t‑test (unequal variances).

meanConfidenceInterval.title: Confidence Interval for a Mean
meanConfidenceInterval.description: Estimation of a population mean from a sample.
meanConfidenceInterval.explanation: Calculates the confidence interval for a mean from sample descriptive statistics (n, mean, standard deviation). Uses Student's t‑distribution.

meanDifferencePower.title: Power for a Mean Difference
meanDifferencePower.description: Calculates the power of a test comparing means.
meanDifferencePower.explanation: Power is the probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is false. This module calculates it from sample sizes, expected difference, and variability.

meanDifferenceSample.title: Sample Size for Mean Difference
meanDifferenceSample.description: Determines the number of subjects needed.
meanDifferenceSample.explanation: Calculates the sample size required to detect a given difference between two means, with specified power and confidence level.

medianPercentileCi.title: Confidence Interval for Median and Percentiles
medianPercentileCi.explanation: Calculates confidence intervals for the median and percentiles (25th, 75th, etc.) using binomial or rank‑based methods.

unmatchedCaseControl.title: Unmatched Case‑Control Analysis
unmatchedCaseControl.description: Analysis of exposure‑disease association in case‑control studies.
unmatchedCaseControl.explanation: This module analyzes data from an unmatched case‑control study. It calculates the odds ratio (OR), its 95% confidence interval, and performs the Mantel‑Haenszel χ² test to test association.

sampleSizeUnmatchedCaseControl.title: Sample Size (Unmatched Case‑Control)
sampleSizeUnmatchedCaseControl.description: Calculates the number of cases and controls needed.
sampleSizeUnmatchedCaseControl.explanation: This module calculates sample size for an unmatched case‑control study. Users enter desired confidence level, power, exposure proportion among controls, and either an odds ratio or exposure proportion among cases.

matchedCaseControl.title: Matched Case‑Control Analysis
matchedCaseControl.explanation: For matched case‑control studies, this module calculates the Mantel‑Haenszel odds ratio, confidence interval, and McNemar's test. It handles 1:1, 1:n, and M:N matching.

cohort.title: Cohort Study Analysis
cohort.description: Calculation of relative risk, risk difference, and attributable fractions.
cohort.explanation: In cohort studies, exposed and non‑exposed groups are followed over time. This module calculates relative risk (RR), risk difference (RD), and etiological fractions among the exposed and in the population.

rct.title: Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT)
rct.description: Specific analysis for randomized clinical trials.
rct.explanation: Module dedicated to randomized trials. Calculates relative risk, absolute risk reduction (ARR), number needed to treat (NNT), and corresponding confidence intervals.

screening.title: Diagnostic Test Evaluation
screening.description: Sensitivity, specificity, predictive values, likelihood ratios.
screening.explanation: This module evaluates a diagnostic test against a gold standard. It calculates sensitivity, specificity, positive and negative predictive values, likelihood ratios, Youden's index, and Cohen's Kappa coefficient.

doseResponse.title: Dose‑Response (χ² Trend)
doseResponse.description: Linear trend test for more than two exposure levels.
doseResponse.explanation: This module performs the Mantel‑Haenszel linear trend χ² test for data with more than two exposure levels (e.g., increasing doses). It calculates a 1‑degree‑of‑freedom p‑value to test the hypothesis of a linear relationship between exposure and risk.
doseResponse.multipleStrata: Multiple strata can be entered to control for confounders.

rByCTable.title: R x C Table
rByCTable.description: Analysis of contingency tables of any size.
rByCTable.explanation: This module generalizes the analysis of 2×2 tables to tables of dimension R (rows) × C (columns). It calculates Pearson χ², likelihood ratio (G²), contingency coefficient, and Cramer's V. It can also analyze multiple strata.
rByCTable.associationBloodGroupDisease: Association between blood group and disease (3 groups × 4 phenotypes)

randomNumbers.title: Random Number Generator
randomNumbers.description: Generation of random numbers for randomization and simulations.
randomNumbers.explanation: This module generates random numbers according to different distributions: uniform, normal, binomial, Poisson. It can also generate sequences for clinical trial randomization (permuted blocks).

simulation.description: Simulate the spread of an infectious disease across interconnected regions, with compartmental models and interventions.
simulation.explanation1: This module allows simulation of an epidemic on a network of regions (e.g., countries, departments). Each region has its own population and compartments (S, E, I, R, D, Q depending on the chosen model). Regions are connected by links that model population flows (mobility), influencing inter‑regional dynamics.
simulation.explanation2: Calculations use ordinary differential equations (ODE) in discrete time. The user can adjust epidemiological parameters, add interventions (lockdown, vaccination), and observe the impact in real time on an interactive map, graphs, or a dynamic network.
simulation.sir: SIR: S, I, R - Basic model without incubation period
simulation.seir: SEIR: S, E, I, R - Adds an exposed phase (E)
simulation.seird: SEIRD: S, E, I, R, D - Includes mortality (D)
simulation.seiqrd: SEIQRD: S, E, I, Q, R, D - Adds quarantine (Q)
simulation.interactiveMap: Interactive map: 2D (Leaflet) or 3D (Globe) with markers proportional to population and mobility flows.
simulation.evolutionCurves: Evolution curves: Tracking S, E, I, R, D compartments over time. Possibility to compare two simulations.
simulation.dynamicGraph: Dynamic graph: Force‑directed visualization, with nodes colored according to infection rate, links weighted by mobility.
simulation.detailedData: Detailed data: Complete list of regions with compartment counts and infection rate.
simulation.massVaccination: Mass vaccination: β reduced, 40% efficacy at day 30
simulation.lateIntervention: Late intervention: lockdown at day 50, R₀=3.0
simulation.cumulativeInterventions: Multiple interventions can be combined (multiplicative effect).
simulation.map: Map: Type: 2D (Leaflet) / 3D (Globe) | Theme: light, dark, satellite | Marker shape: circle / icon | Line opacity (mobility)
simulation.graphs: Graphs: Line style: solid / dashed | Adjustable thickness | Fill opacity under curves | Grid show/hide | Annotations for interventions
simulation.network: Network: Node shape: circle, square, triangle | Link style: solid / dashed | Repulsion force (charge) | Region clustering (based on cluster attribute)
simulation.step1: Select a model (SEIRD by default) and a scenario (Base).
simulation.step2: Click Start to launch the simulation. Watch the evolution on the map and graphs.
simulation.step3: Add an intervention (lockdown) via the dedicated button to see its impact on the infected curve.
simulation.step4: Switch to comparison mode to simulate an alternative scenario with different parameters and compare curves.
simulation.keyboardShortcuts: Keyboard shortcuts (3D map mode): arrows to rotate globe, R to recenter, 2/3 to change projection.
simulation.technicalNote: Note: The simulation uses a discrete time step of 1 day. Equations are based on standard compartmental models. Inter‑region mobility is modeled by a diffusion term proportional to the difference in prevalence.

geospatial.description: Based on Leaflet and react‑leaflet – explore your epidemiological data on dynamic maps.
geospatial.explanation1: The geospatial module allows loading, visualizing, and analyzing georeferenced data (points, incidents, incidence rates…). It offers three complementary representation modes: individual markers, clustering for large datasets, and heatmap to visualize densities.
geospatial.explanation2: Data can be imported from CSV or Excel files, or via built‑in examples (historical epidemics, continental data). An integrated AI analysis automatically provides summaries, trends, and alerts.
geospatial.dataImport: Data import: Drag and drop or select your files. Automatic detection of latitude/longitude columns and intensity value.
geospatial.multiLayers: Multi‑layers: Each dataset is an independent layer. Show/hide, change color, radius or opacity of each series.
geospatial.integratedAIAnalysis: Integrated AI analysis: Automatically generate a summary, trends, recommendations, and risk alerts from the visible data.
geospatial.individualPoints: Individual points: Each data point is represented by a circle whose size and color vary according to intensity (e.g., number of cases). Ideal for small datasets.
geospatial.pointsParameters: Parameters: global radius, opacity, color per dataset
geospatial.clustering: Clustering: Automatically groups nearby points into clusters whose size indicates the number of elements. Recommended for over 500 points.
geospatial.clusteringOptions: Options: cluster color, text color, max cluster radius, zoom‑out threshold
geospatial.heatmap: Heatmap: Visualizes point density weighted by intensity. Ideal for identifying clusters or risk areas.
geospatial.heatmapSettings: Settings: radius, blur, color gradient, minimum opacity
geospatial.heatmapConfig: Heatmap: Radius – heat point size (default 25) | Blur – gradient smoothing (default 15) | Minimum opacity – transparency of cold areas | Customizable gradient – e.g., 0.4 → blue, 1.0 → red
geospatial.clusteringConfig: Clustering: Cluster color and text color | Max cluster radius (grouping distance) | Zoom‑out threshold – from which zoom level clusters are shown as individual points | Spiderfy – spider arrangement at max zoom
geospatial.note: Note: Each dataset retains its own visibility, clustering, heatmap, color, radius, and opacity settings. Global settings serve as defaults.
geospatial.summary: Summary: Description of clusters, correlations, global trends
geospatial.insights: Insights: Key findings: risk area, increase, under‑reporting
geospatial.alerts: Alerts: Risk level (low / moderate / high) and points of attention
geospatial.step1: Load a CSV file containing latitude, longitude, and cases columns.
geospatial.step2: Enable clustering to visualize the global distribution, then switch to heatmap to identify hotspots.
geospatial.step3: In the configuration panel, adjust dataset color, point radius, and opacity.
geospatial.step4: Click Analyze to get an AI report with risk level and recommendations.

workspace.description: Run R code directly in your browser.
workspace.explanation1: The workspace embeds a full R engine based on WebR (R port to WebAssembly). You can create, open, and save multiple .R scripts, run all code or a single line, and immediately view results in the console or as graphics.
workspace.explanation2: The following features are available: script execution, file management, interactive console, graph capture, image download, installation of common packages (ggplot2, dplyr, …). Some parts (advanced animation, full support for all packages) are still being improved and marked as beta.
workspace.multiFileEditor: Multi‑file editor: Tabs to manage multiple scripts simultaneously. Rename, duplicate, delete via context menu. Auto‑save in browser (localStorage).
workspace.interactiveConsoleFeature: Interactive console: Enter R commands live, navigate history with arrows, view text output and graphics in the same area.
workspace.graphicalVisualizations: Graphical visualizations: All graphs generated by R are automatically captured and displayed. Grid mode, animation mode, individual download, and full‑screen expansion.
workspace.packageManagement: Package management: Install packages from the webR repository (e.g., ggplot2, dplyr, KernSmooth) via the dedicated field in the header or directly with webr::install().
workspace.animationBetaDetail: Animation (beta): If your script produces multiple graphs (e.g., in a loop), you can view them in grid mode or start an animation. The button allows play/pause. Manual navigation is also possible.
workspace.animationNote: animation works for pre‑generated sequences, but continuous playback of dynamic graphs is still being optimized.
workspace.packageInstallationDetail: Package installation: WebR allows installation of packages pre‑compiled for WebAssembly. In the header, a dedicated field lets you quickly install a package (e.g., ggplot2). Installation logs appear in the console.
workspace.packageNote: Packages requiring system dependencies or native compilation may not be available.
workspace.codeExample:
# Mobile epidemic curve
x <- seq(-5, 10, length.out = 200)
for (i in 1:40) {
  mu <- i / 8
  y <- dnorm(x, mean = mu, sd = 1.5)
  plot(x, y, type="n", ylim=c(0, 0.3), axes=FALSE)
  polygon(c(x, rev(x)), c(y, rep(0, 200)), col="#3b82f622", border=NA)
  lines(x, y, col="#3b82f6", lwd=3)
  Sys.sleep(0.04)
}
workspace.step3: Click the Run button to execute the whole script. You will see an animation appear in the console.
workspace.step4: Use navigation arrows or the button to control the animation. Export an image by clicking the icon.

explorer.description: Directly access over 35 million biomedical references from your workspace.
explorer.explanation1: The explorer integrates a complete interface to the PubMed (NCBI) database. You can search by keywords, filter by year and publication type, sort by relevance or date, and view detailed abstracts.
explorer.explanation2: A unique MeSH query generation feature uses PubMed's automatic translation to enrich your search terms with appropriate MeSH descriptors. Articles can be saved locally (favorites) and exported in RIS format for your bibliographic management software.
explorer.advancedSearch: Advanced search: Keywords, period filters (e.g., 2010‑2025), article types (review, clinical trial, meta‑analysis…), sort by relevance or publication date.
explorer.meshGenerator: MeSH generator: From a free expression, retrieve the MeSH translation suggested by PubMed, including controlled terms and field tags (e.g., "[PTYP]").
explorer.favoritesExport: Favorites & export: Locally save up to 200 articles. Export a page of results or all favorites in RIS format (compatible with EndNote, Zotero, Mendeley).
explorer.home: Home: Search bar with filter | Direct access to favorites from the link at the bottom | Display MeSH suggestions in a separate panel
explorer.results: Results: Quick toggle between search and favorites | Article detail sidebar (abstract, authors, DOI, PMID) | Temporal distribution chart of results
explorer.step1: In the centered mode, type covid-19 vaccine.
explorer.step2: Click Generate MeSH to get an optimized query (e.g., "COVID-19 Vaccines"[Mesh]). Apply it.
explorer.step4: Browse the results, click an article to see details. Add it to favorites (bookmark icon).
explorer.step5: Switch to Favorites tab, then Export all to generate a RIS file.

analysis.title: AI Epidemiological Analysis
analysis.description: Intelligent analysis will be available soon — automatic insights, cluster detection, and epidemiological recommendations.

settings.timelineDescription: Timeline slider — real data to be connected.
settings.comparisonHint: Load several examples then compare their indicators.
settings.realtimeDescription: Will simulate receiving live data from a surveillance API.

map.emptyDescription: Import a CSV/Excel or load an epidemiological example via the "Examples" button.

modal.title: Epidemiological examples
modal.description: Documented real data — WHO, Johns Hopkins, CEREMUJER, and other organizations.

toasts.formatError: Unsupported format — CSV or Excel only

empty_state.title: Start a simulation
empty_state.description: Choose a real‑world disease example or a scenario to start the simulation and visualize epidemic spread.

charts.empty: Launch a simulation to see graphs.

data_modal.title: Import data
data_modal.csv_label: CSV — regions (columns: id, name, population, S, E, I, R, D, latitude, longitude, color, connections, cluster)

explanation_modal.title: Documentation
explanation_modal.slider_body: Drag the day 0–365 slider to visualize the epidemic state at any day. The map and dashboard update instantly. Use Play to animate the progression.
explanation_modal.models_body: SIR, SEIR, SEIRD, SEIQRD. The full trajectory is precomputed on parameter change — no flicker during playback.
explanation_modal.rt_body: Rt (effective reproduction number) is calculated as R0 × S/N × (1 − reductions). Rt < 1 means the epidemic is under control.
explanation_modal.phase_body: The S–I graph plots the trajectory in S × I space. The characteristic arc shape is the signature of a well‑parameterized epidemic model.
explanation_modal.interventions_body: Each intervention reduces β by a percentage, applied from the start day. Effects are cumulative (capped at 95%).
explanation_modal.comparison_body: The "Compare" button launches a parallel simulation with different parameters. Curves are overlaid.

examples_modal.title: Disease examples
examples_modal.subtitle: Parameters and initial conditions drawn from real data

covid19.name: COVID-19
covid19.description: Global pandemic. Airborne transmission, incubation period 5–6 days, CFR ~1%.

ebola2014.name: Ebola Outbreak
ebola2014.description: Major health crisis in West Africa. CFR ~50%. Direct contact transmission.

flu_seasonal.name: Seasonal Flu
flu_seasonal.description: Classic winter epidemic. Moderate R0, rapid recovery, low overall fatality.

measles.name: Measles
measles.description: Highly contagious disease (R0 12–18). Effective vaccine but insufficient coverage in some areas.

meningitis.name: Meningococcal Meningitis
meningitis.description: Seasonal epidemics in the sub‑Saharan "meningitis belt". Fatality ~10%.

cholera.name: Cholera
cholera.description: Epidemics linked to contaminated water. Rapid mortality without treatment, low with rehydration.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.subtitle: Wepisia
privacy.summary: Wepisia is an open‑source tool for epidemiology and biostatistics. This policy explains how we handle your data.
privacy.noDataDesc: Wepisia does not collect, store, or transmit any personal data. All calculations are performed locally in your browser.
privacy.localDesc: The data you enter (table values, simulation parameters) remain on your device and are deleted when you close the tab.
privacy.noTrackingDesc: No third‑party cookies, no analytics scripts, no ads. Your privacy is fully respected.
privacy.clearLocalStorage: You can clear this data at any time via your browser settings.
privacy.dataUsageDesc: The only data you enter is used for calculations.
privacy.calculationsDesc: The values you enter (e.g., counts, parameters) are only used to generate the displayed results. They are never transmitted to our servers.
privacy.persistenceDesc: No data is retained after you close the tab. You can also manually clear stored preferences.
privacy.cookiesDesc: The application does not use cookies.
privacy.cookiesDetail: Only local storage (localStorage) may be used to remember your language preferences and interface state. This data is not cookies and is never shared.
privacy.thirdPartiesDesc: No information is shared with third parties.
privacy.thirdPartiesDetail: The application contains no external scripts (analytics, ads) and does not transmit any data. The only network requests involve loading necessary resources (JavaScript libraries, stylesheets).
privacy.securityDesc: Your data remains under your control.
privacy.localComputationDesc: All calculations happen on your machine. No data leaves your browser.
privacy.httpsDesc: The site is served over HTTPS, ensuring the integrity of downloaded files.
privacy.changesDesc: Any modifications will be published on this page.
privacy.changesDetail: This policy may be updated occasionally. The last update date is at the top of the page. We encourage you to review it periodically.
privacy.contactDesc: For any questions about this policy.

about.title: About
about.subtitle: Wepisia
about.intro: A free tool for epidemiology, designed for accessibility in low‑resource settings
about.visionDesc: In developing countries, resources are often limited. Wepisia was designed to meet these realities: local processing, no installation, multilingual, and open source (MIT).
about.openEpiDesc: OpenEpi revolutionized access to epidemiological calculations by making them free, online, and accessible to all. Published under the MIT license, it remains a global reference.
about.commitmentDesc: Wepisia is and will remain free, open source (MIT), and multilingual. Developed by Xcept-Health, it embodies our belief: public health tools must be accessible without barriers.
about.openEpiHeritageDesc: Context, goals, and features of the original project.
about.openEpiContext: OpenEpi arose from the observation that commercial software did not always meet epidemiologists' needs. The team aimed to provide modern, documented, tested programs with a common interface, running online or locally, platform-independent, and open source.
about.openEpiGoals: Goals: provide modern, platform-independent, open-source software; foster interaction between epidemiologists and developers.
about.feature1: Stratified tables, screening tests, diagnostic method evaluation
about.feature2: Power calculations for cohort, case-control, clinical trials
about.feature3: Random number generator, chi-square trend test (dose-response)
about.feature4: Result file saving and loading of sample data
about.genesisText: Initiated by F. Ariel Shadrac OUEDRAOGO, a medical student and self-taught developer from Burkina Faso, Wepisia is an African evolution of OpenEpi. Completely redesigned with modern technologies (React, Vite, Tailwind), it brings:
about.featureSimulation: Advanced multi-region simulation (SIR, SEIR, SEIRD, SEIQRD)
about.featureMultilingual: Full multilingual support (Mooré, Wolof, Hausa, Swahili, French, English)
about.recognitionDesc: We wish to express our deep gratitude to the creators of OpenEpi
about.recognitionConclusion: Without their pioneering work, Wepisia would never have existed. Their source code, available under the MIT license, remains the scientific foundation on which our project rests.
about.contributeDesc: Developers, epidemiologists, translators, designers: all contributions are welcome!

notFound.title: Lost in the void?
notFound.message: This page has drifted off our radar. Use your instruments to return to safe harbor.
notFound.modalText: These coordinates lead to the Martyrs' Monument in Ouagadougou, Burkina Faso – a memorial to those who sacrificed their lives for freedom. A place of remembrance and hope.

twoByTwo.title: 2×2 Table
twoByTwo.description: Compute odds ratio, relative risk, and chi‑square tests.
twoByTwo.increasedRisk: The relative risk indicates increased risk among the exposed.
twoByTwo.protectiveEffect: The relative risk indicates a protective effect of exposure.
twoByTwo.noAssociation: The relative risk equals 1, suggesting no association.
twoByTwo.ciNote: CI for OR and RR are computed using Taylor series approximation (Woolf method).
twoByTwo.orNote: CI computed using Taylor series (Woolf method). AFp|OR = (a/c₁)·(OR−1)/OR.
twoByTwo.principleText: The 2×2 table analysis studies the association between exposure and disease. It computes odds ratio (case‑control studies) and relative risk (cohort studies), along with various independence tests.
twoByTwo.fisherDesc: Based on hypergeometric distribution, recommended for small samples.
twoByTwo.interpretationText: If the OR or RR confidence interval excludes 1, the association is significant. The chi‑square p‑value indicates the significance of the association (p < 0.05).
